coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as fluorescent probe for “off–on” Detecting sodium dodecyl-benzenesulfonate in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Huilin
Liao, Xiufen
Wu, Qingyi
Xie, Xiangli
Zhong, Fuxin
Yi, Zhongsheng
Qin, Mei
Wu, Zhenli
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we propose an “off–on” approach for the detection of sodium dodecyl-benzenesulfonate (SDBS) using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probe. We firstly demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of ruthenium (Ru), and the system was thus “turn-off”. The resulting CDs-Ru system was found to be sensitive to SDBS, SDBS not only serves to shelter the CDs effectively from being quenched, but also to reverse the quenching and restore the fluorescence due to its ability to remove Ru from the surface of CDs (turn-on). An eco-friendly, simple and sensitive platform for the detection of SDBS based on the CDs-Ru probes has been proposed. After the experimental conditions were optimized, the linear range for detection SDBS was 0.10–7.50μg/mL, with correlation coefficient (r) 0.9988, detection limit was 0.033μg/mL (3σ). This method is facile, rapid, low cost, environment-friendly, and possesses the potential for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ru
Dodecyl-benzenesulfonate
“Off–on” fluorescence probe

Determination of SDBS in real water samples

B. The Stern–Volmer curves of Ru quenched CDs at different temperatures.

Effects of the SDBS on the fluorescence of CDs-Ru system.
Effect of SDBS on the fluorescence of CDs.
Linear relationship for detection of SDBS.
Schematic illustration for the “off–on” detection of Ru and SDBS by CDs fluorescence probe.
Ability of interfering substances to recovery the fluorescence of CDs-Ru system.
Determination of SDBS concentration in river water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as fluorescent probe for “off–on” Detecting sodium dodecyl-benzenesulfonate in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Scientific Experiment Center of Mining, Metallurgy and Environment, College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Scientific Experiment Center of Mining

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region Testing Institute of Product Quality, Nanning 530007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region Testing Institute of Product Quality

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we propose an “off–on” approach for the detection of sodium dodecyl-benzenesulfonate (SDBS) using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probe. We firstly demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of ruthenium (Ru), and the system was thus “turn-off”. The resulting CDs-Ru system was found to be sensitive to SDBS, SDBS not only serves to shelter the CDs effectively from being quenched, but also to reverse the quenching and restore the fluorescence due to its ability to remove Ru from the surface of CDs (turn-on). An eco-friendly, simple and sensitive platform for the detection of SDBS based on the CDs-Ru probes has been proposed. After the experimental conditions were optimized, the linear range for detection SDBS was 0.10–7.50μg/mL, with correlation coefficient (r) 0.9988, detection limit was 0.033μg/mL (3σ). This method is facile, rapid, low cost, environment-friendly, and possesses the potential for practical application.
Scheme 1. Schematic illustration for the “off–on” detection of Ru and SDBS by CDs fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:para: Dodecyl-benzenesulfonate (SDBS) is the most used anionic surfactants in various industrial, household and commercial applications. More and more SDBS, which is one of main ingredient of detergents, has entered the environment by means of waste water. The concentration range of linear alkylbenzene sulfonates in WWTP was in the range of 2.4–6.7mg/L and for effluent was 19,000–71,000ng/L [1,2]. In rivers sediment, the average concentrations of SDBS reported were 780ng/g [3]. The continuous inputs of SDBS into the environment, although such compounds biodegrade rapidly, may affect biological cell membranes and enzyme activities by binding to some groups of proteins and cells, resulting in toxic symptoms of organs and animal and human organisms. Thus, the development of a simple method to eco-friendly, and sensitively detect Ru and SDBS has received a lot of attention. In recent years, many methods for SDBS analysis have been proposed, such as spectroscopic analysis [4–8], gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC/MS) [9], gas chromatography coupled with flame ionization detector (GC-FID) [10], and capillary electrophoresis coupled with mass spectrometry (CE-MS) [11]. Unfortunately, these methods require complicated operation procedures and expensive instrumentation.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of harmless and eco-friendly fluorescent nanometersized particles. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CDs are easier to synthesis, remarkably cheaper in handling and production costs. For synthesizing CDs, there are two fundamental approaches, called “top-down” [12–14] and “bottom-up” [15–17] routes. Top-down strategy generally refers to the use of physical or chemical methods to cut bulk carbonaceous materials (carbon nanotubes, graphite, carbon soot, and activated carbon) into CDs, while the bottom-up strategy involves the condensation and carbonization of different carbon precursors, such as citric acid, carbohydrates, and graphite [18]. Fengxiang Wang et al. [14] “top-down” prepared blue fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via the hydrothermal method using reoxidized graphene oxide, and a direct fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cu2+ was established based on the as prepared GQDs. Changhui Fu et al. [17] reported a “bottom-up” strategy for scalable production carbon dots through the chemical aerosol flow method. The as prepared CDs are suited for cellular imaging because of photostability and biocompatibility. To date, CDs are an ideal material used as fluorescent probes. The number of analytical applications for these carbonaceous nanomaterials had been described, related to metal ions (specially, Hg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+ and Sn2+) [19–23], anion [24], biomolecule [25–28], and pollutants [29,30]. To the best of our knowledge, CDs used as a sensitive fluorescence probes for “off–on” sensing SDBS have not been reported yet.

In this paper, eco-friendly CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method and had been utilized as an “off–on” fluorescence probe for the detection of SDBS. This method is facile, low cost, and environmental-friendly. The simple detection strategy is presented in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the formation of CDs-Ru ground state complex results in the florescence quench of CDs, the fluorescence probe “turn-off”. Then the self-assembly of SDBS on CD surfaces occurs, leading to the competition between Ru and SDBS, and disturbs the interaction between Ru and CDs, Ru was removed from the surface of CDs, the fluorescence of CDs recovers, and the system thus could be “turn on”. The fluorescence recovery intensity of CDs was in linear relationship with the concentration of SDBS, which makes it possible to detect SDBS. This proposed fluorescence probe showed a wide linear detection range, good selectivity, and low detection limit.

A 1.0mg·mL−1 stock solution of Ru (III) was prepared by dissolving 0.0223g of pure ruthenium in 20mL HCl of 2.0mol·L−1 and then diluted to 100mL with deionized water. A 100μg·mL solution of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China) was prepared by dissolving 0.0100g of SDBS and then diluted to 100mL with deionized water. All reagents are of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions are prepared by ultrapure water and the ultrapure water is produced by a Millipore-Q water system.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature with a RF-5301 PC spectra fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were performed using a Carry50 UV–vis spectrophoto-meter (Varian, USA). All pH were measured with a pHS-3C pH precision pH meter (Shanghai ray magnetic instrument plant, China). Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were obtained on a JEM-2100F Transmission electron microscope with 120kV acceleration voltage. Infrared spectrum of CDs was detected by a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Thermo, USA).

In order to obtain homogeneous solution, the pristine CDs were synthesized from sodium citrate through a simple, convenient and one-step hydrothermal method [19]. In brief, 0.2g sodium citrate, 1.5g NH4HCO3 and 10mL double-distilled water were put into a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave, respectively. Successively, the mixture was hydrothermally treated for 4h at 180°C in a drying oven. After the reaction was finished, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. For purification, the product was conducted through a dialysis tube (1000Da, molecular weight cut off) in the dark for about 24h, the purified homogeneous CDs were thus obtained, which were stable for several months. The final product was re-dissolved to 100mL in double-distilled water and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Ru and SDBS
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for SDBS detection is described as follows: 30μL CDs, a specific volume of Ru (with a final concentration of Ru 16.00ng/mL) and different amounts of SDBS were added into a 5mL colorimetric tube, and diluted into a volume of 5mL with double-distilled water. SDBS final concentration was ranged from 0.10 to 7.50μg/mL. Fluorescence spectra were measured at room temperature at λex =350nm, λem =450nm. Both excitation and emission slit width were 5nm.

The as-prepared CDs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and FT-IR spectra. To evaluate the optical properties of CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 1A) of CDs were investigated. According to previous reports, no obvious absorption band of CDs can be observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. While in our experiment, the maximal absorption of CDs was at 340nm, which is much different from most of previous reports [31–33]. Fluorescence spectrum of CDs showed a 450nm emission peak under the excitation of 350nm, with a Stokes shift of 110nm. It should be noted that when excited at 350nm, the sodium citrate solution itself was non-emission, suggesting the bright blue fluorescence comes from CDs. When excited at 350nm, the full width at half maximum of CDs emission spectrum was about 65nm, which was narrower than those reported elsewhere (>70nm) [21,34,35]. The quantum yield (Φu) of CDs in our experiments was calculated by using quinine sulfate (Φs =0.55) as the reference standard [36]
Φ u
=
Φ s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
where Fu and FS are the areas under the fluorescence curves of CDs and standard, respectively. Au and AS are absorbance of the sample and standard at the excitation wavelength, respectively. The Φu value of CDs was found to be 68%.

TEM image was used to qualitatively study the morphology of CDs. Fig. 1B shows that CDs were monodispersed and displayed individual particles. The sizes of CDs were around 3.5nm, providing a basis for the following study. The above results demonstrated that CDs were spherical morphology, homogeneous distribution and maintained good performance in terms of photoluminescence. CD fluorescence mechanism has not been completely understood, may cause by the radiative recombination of surface energy trap excitons of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1C, FT-IR spectrum was acquired to study the functional groups of CDs. The peak at 3447cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups, while the peak at 1636cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of carbonyl group, the symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate anions in 1360cm−1 are also observed. The results reveal that the as-prepared CDs were mainly surrounded by carboxylate and hydroxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of CDs by SDBS
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the fluorescence signal of CDs response to Ru, the fluorescence change of CDs upon the addition of Ru was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2A, when CDs were free in aqueous solution, they showed strong fluorescence intensity, while it was noticeable that the fluorescence of CDs decreased sharply upon the addition of Ru. According to the data, over 83% fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched when the final concentration of Ru was 0.30μmol/L. CD fluorescence intensity did not decrease after the addition of Ru for about 2min, and a further increase of time did not lead to any further detectable quenching of the fluorescence of CDs, indicating that the quenching kinetics was fairly fast.

It was found that the quenching effect of Ru on the fluorescence of CDs was concentration-dependent and could be described by a Stern–Volmer equation [37,38].
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
C
= 1 +
τ 0
K q
C
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Ru, respectively. Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant, which is a quenching efficiency measurement, and [C] is the concentration of quencher. τ0 is the life time of fluorophore (taken as 10−8 s), Kq is quenching rate constants, and it could be calculated by the equation Kq =Ksv/τ0.

In order to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the interaction of SDBS with CDs was studied by spectrofluorometry at three temperatures (288K, 298K, 308K) (Fig. 2B). According to Fig. 2B, the corresponding Ksv values at 288, 298 and 308K are 2.49×107, 2.03×107, and 1.75×107 L/M respectively. Thus, the calculated Kq values at 288, 298 and 308K are 2.49×1015, 2.03×1015, and 1.75×1015 M−1 s−1, respectively. In general, the maximum collisional quenching constant (Kq) is 2.0×1010 M−1 s−1. In our experiments, Kq of CDs quenching procedure initiated by Ru are much greater than 2.0×1010 M−1 s−1, and Ksv inversely correlated with temperature. It can be concluded that the quenching process is probably by static quenching resulting from the formation of CDs-Ru complex.

UV–vis absorption measurement is a simple method to explore the structural changes of compound [39,40]. In order to further verify the quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of Ru were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 , fix concentration CDs, the addition of Ru led to a gradual decrease in CDs absorption, while keeping the location of the peak unchanged, suggesting the interaction between Ru and CDs in the ground state through complex formation, which in accordance with the result of Stern–Volmer curve.
We thus conjecture that the formation of a ground state complex between CDs and Ru results in fluorescence quenching of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of CDs by SDBS

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of critical parameters including pH, time needed for growth of fluorescence intensity, and Ru concentrations has been investigated in order to establish the optimum analytical conditions for the detection of SDBS. Controlling the concentration of Ru is a critical parameter that should be taken into the consideration in order to improve the sensitivity and the linear range of the method. When the concentration of Ru is too low, there is a low quenching efficiency of CDs, resulted in the high detection background and relatively narrow fluorescence recovery range, which leading to the narrow detection range. However, when Ru concentration is too high, the fluorescence enhancement efficiency starts to be decreased. The negative effect of the higher concentration of Ru is probably due to the increasing in collisional encounter that lead to increasing the possibility of collisional quenching of released CDs. Taking all above situations into account, the concentration of 0.18μmol/L Ru was chosen as the optimum concentration in which we can get relatively wide linear range and high sensitively. Besides, the influence of pH, reaction medium and temperature was investigated. Experiment results showed that, at pH6.5–7.4 water medium can obtain high recovery fluorescence intensity, the ultra pure water was thus selected as the reaction medium for the further experiments. The reaction between CDs-Ru and SDBS reached the equilibrium in room temperature within 2min, and the fluorescence of CDs-Ru-SDBS system was stable for about 1.0h.

Under the optimum conditions, the linear range for the determination of SDBS was evaluated. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was enhanced along with elevating the concentrations of analyte. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs-Ru and the concentration of SDBS is shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence enhancement can also be described by the following equations: ΔIF
=19.91 C+8.96, (ΔIF
= F − F0 ). The enhancement of fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of SDBS in the range from 0.10 to 7.50μg/mL (r=0.9988). Detection limit for the determination of SDBS was 0.033μg/mL (3σ/k).

To test the ability of the proposed method for the analysis of SDBS in real samples, when fix the concentration of SDBS 0.35μg/mL, a tolerable error within ± 5.0%, the interference of foreign substances were evaluated. Results were shown in Table 1 , it can be seen that the relatively high amounts of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, CTMAB, PVA, Twain 40, Twain 80, and Triton X-100 have little interference. All these promote the high selectivity of the proposed platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SDBS in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The operating applicability of the proposed sensing system in real samples was investigated by the determination of SDBS in river water samples. The real samples were obtained from different rivers, samples were resting a night to make the impurities in the water precipitate completely. 500μL sample solution was used to perform based on the procedure described in Section 2.3. The SDBS contents in river water samples were derived from the standard curve and the regression equation, average recovery test was performed by using the standard addition method, analytical data were summarized in Table 2 . As we can see from Table 2, the recoveries of this “off–on” sensing system were in the ranged from 98.0% to 104.0%, with RSD less than 4.25%, indicating satisfied recovery and precision of the proposed method. Therefore, the new “off–on” sensing platform provided a simple and convenient way to analysis SDBS directly in river water samples.

In conclusion, CDs have been used as an eco-friendly, simple, and sensitive fluorescent probes for “off–on” detection of dodecyl-benzenesulfonate in aqueous solution. Ru can quench the fluorescence of CDs through forming a ground state complex between CDs and Ru. Upon the addition of SDBS into CDs-Ru, the fluorescence can be recovered. The likely mechanism relies upon SDBS being able to effectively shelter the quenching due to the self-assembly of SDBS on carbon surfaces occurs, and the competition between Ru, SDBS on the surface of CDs disturbs the interaction between Ru and CDs. Under the optimized conditions, linear regression equation shows the linear range of SDBS was 0.10 to 7.50μg/mL, with detection limits of 0.033μg/mL. The newly construction platform is facile, rapid, low cost, and environment friendly, therefore it was employed to detect SDBS in river water samples by using the standard addition method, the recovery was found to be 98.0% to 104.0% with RSD less than 4.25%. The excellent performance of the proposed CDs-based “off–on” fluorescence probe shows that this method possesses the potential for practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support received from Guangxi Scientific Experiment Center of Mining, Metallurgy and Environment (KH2012ZD008) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61264007 and 21267008).
